current_fact.fact_text: On April 21, 2014, Flint, Michigan switched its drinking water source from treated Lake Huron water to the Flint River as a cost-saving measure. Improper treatment corroded old pipes, leaching lead into households and turning a budget decision into a long-running public health catastrophe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

current_fact.match_reason: The Flint crisis is partly a story of institutional failure to learn from warnings; an episode about learning, mistakes, and systemic change provides a reflective counterpoint.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1965, the 1964–1965 New York World’s Fair opened for its second and final season, offering visitors a shimmering preview of a technological future filled with picturephones, space travel, and automated homes. It became a cultural time capsule of mid‑century optimism about science and progress.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

queue.match_reason: The World’s Fair showcased futuristic machines and pop spectacle, echoed by an episode celebrating the machine-driven shimmer of disco and synthpop decades later.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 1977, the musical "Annie" opened on Broadway, turning a Depression-era comic strip orphan into a global icon of optimism. Its now-classic tune "Tomorrow" became a kind of musical shorthand for stubborn hope in hard times.

episode.title: Morning Drops #09 - Birthday Cake
episode.description: Today's breakfast comes with a sprinkle of stardust and a slice of birthday cake as we celebrate a pure and unique soul, Yasmin. On Sedna, we may not do years, but we sure know how to party!

queue.match_reason: A bright, hopeful Broadway debut about new tomorrows aligns with a celebratory, stardust-sprinkled birthday episode full of theater-ready joy.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 1982, Milwaukee Brewers pitcher Rollie Fingers became the first in Major League Baseball history to record 300 saves. His handlebar mustache and late‑inning calm helped define the very idea of a modern relief ace.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: A record-setting closer embodies momentum and finishing strong, matching an upbeat, on‑the‑move mix built for pushing through the late innings of your day.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1989, around 100,000 students gathered in Beijing’s Tiananmen Square to mourn reform leader Hu Yaobang, unintentionally igniting weeks of pro‑democracy protests. The sea of handwritten banners and hunger strikers transformed a memorial into a mass movement the world would not forget.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A vast student protest demanding political change pairs with an episode centered on one of rock’s most explicitly political bands, both merging sound, anger, and hope.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 1987, a powerful car bomb exploded in Colombo, Sri Lanka, killing 106 people; authorities blamed the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam. It was one of the deadliest urban bombings in the country’s civil war, revealing how cities themselves can become front lines.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: A brutal act in a long conflict sits beside an episode about Flamenco, a genre born from historical pain and marginalization, both hinting at how cultures process violence through art.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 2019, eight coordinated bombings struck churches and hotels across Sri Lanka on Easter Sunday, killing at least 269 people. The attacks shattered a decade of relative peace and reverberated through island communities and diasporas worldwide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A tragic day of bombings tied to a religious holiday calls for the kind of reflective, healing space that this episode creates through slow storytelling and cinematic sound.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2021, the Indonesian submarine KRI Nanggala (402) sank during a torpedo drill in the Bali Sea, killing all 53 crew members. The tragedy underscored both the hidden dangers of deep‑sea operations and the quiet bravery of those who disappear beneath the surface.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A submarine loss in deep water connects to an episode built around diving and immersion, offering a gentler, contemplative sonic descent after a somber maritime story.

queue.fact_text: On April 21 in the final days of World War II, Soviet forces attacking from the south struck at the German High Command’s underground headquarters near Zossen, south of Berlin. The assault helped collapse the communications nerve center of the Nazi war machine just days before the city fell.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: The collapse of a militaristic regime is juxtaposed with an episode celebrating women claiming space and volume in rock, both telling stories of challenging rigid power structures.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 1806, during the Napoleonic Wars, a lone French frigate managed to escape a superior British squadron off the coast of South Africa in what became known as the Action of 21 April 1806. The running sea fight turned into a lesson in how wind, weather, and nerve can let a smaller ship slip past a larger threat.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A daring frigate escape on rough seas resonates with an episode literally set on a boat, both savoring the mix of risk, navigation, and ocean-wide possibility.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 1796, Napoleon Bonaparte decisively defeated the army of Piedmont at the Battle of Mondovì during his Montenotte Campaign. Within a week, Piedmont surrendered, reshaping the map of northern Italy and announcing a young general’s startling rise.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: An Italian battlefield that shifted the country’s fate pairs with an Italian-focused episode about North–South energies, both tracing how the peninsula’s contrasts shape its stories.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 1809, Napoleon’s forces pushed two Austrian army corps out of Landshut while other French corps held off the main Austrian army to the north, setting up his victory at Eckmühl. It was a day of rapidly shifting fronts, couriers, and improvisation across the Bavarian countryside.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A pivotal campaign day that helped ‘launch’ an empire mirrors Sedna FM’s explosive first episode, both marking where an expansive story truly begins.

queue.fact_text: Long before Napoleon, on April 21, 43 BC, Mark Antony was again defeated near Mutina by forces loyal to the Roman Senate under Aulus Hirtius. Although Hirtius died in the fighting and Decimus Brutus was soon murdered, the clash accelerated the end of the Roman Republic and the rise of a new order.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: Roman power struggles birthed new political identities and ‘masks’; an episode about musical alter-egos riffs on how personas shift when empires and egos are at stake.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 1964, a violent tornado ripped through the Illinois town of Belvidere, striking the high school just as students were boarding buses. This single event produced over 500 casualties in minutes and became a key case study for school safety in severe weather.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

queue.match_reason: A town forever marked by a sudden storm contrasts with an episode about music that endures calmly across generations—a quiet nod to what survives past disaster.

queue.fact_text: Also on April 21, 1967, another tornado from the same outbreak devastated Oak Lawn, a suburb of Chicago, as it barreled through rush‑hour traffic and busy streets. Its path of mangled cars and shattered storefronts showed how urban layouts shape the human cost of natural disasters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: A tornado slicing through a dense urban grid echoes an episode about a different industrial city’s edges and stories, both revealing how streets and people shape each other.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 2010, the Kharkiv Pact was signed in Ukraine granting Russia an extension of its Black Sea Fleet lease in Crimea for 25 years beyond 2017. In exchange, Ukraine received gas discounts—binding energy, geography, and politics into one contested document later voided amid crisis.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A treaty negotiated in one Eastern European city is contrasted with an episode walking through London, another global crossroads where power, energy, and everyday life quietly intersect.

queue.fact_text: On April 21, 1958, United Nations Security Council debates around Kashmir produced Resolution 47, envisioning demilitarization and a free vote that never fully materialized. Decades later, its unfulfilled blueprint still haunts discussions of borders, self‑determination, and the limits of international promises.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A long-unresolved conflict and an unrealized plan are set against an episode inviting slowness and contemplation, suggesting the need to breathe before drawing new lines on maps.

published.fact_text: On April 21, 1972, Apollo 16 astronauts John Young and Charles Duke piloted the lunar module Orion down to the Moon’s Descartes Highlands, the fifth crewed landing in human history. Their three moonwalks helped prove that even rugged highland terrain could be explored on foot and by lunar rover, expanding our idea of where humans could travel off‑world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal journey into space pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space, underscored by forward-driving, exploratory sounds.

published.fact_text: In 1964, the Transit-5BN-3 navigation satellite failed to reach orbit; as it burnt up in the atmosphere, its SNAP nuclear power source scattered around 0.95 kg of plutonium worldwide. The mishap became a formative cautionary tale in the early nuclear-space age, shaping how we think about risk in off‑planet technology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Both the satellite failure and the episode question the unseen costs of our technological systems, from nuclear hardware in orbit to phones in our pockets.

published.fact_text: On April 21, 1946, a tornado near Timber Lake, South Dakota was recorded as being roughly 4 miles (6.4 km) wide, making it among the broadest tornadoes ever documented. This atmospheric giant highlighted how extreme and surprising the geometry of Earth’s storms can be.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A colossal tornado feels like an atmospheric electric shock, well mirrored by the episode’s stormy guitars and high-voltage rock energy.

published.fact_text: The tornado outbreak of April 21, 1967 carved a deadly path across Illinois, killing over 50 people and injuring more than 1,000. One violent twister struck Belvidere High School during dismissal, turning a routine school day into one of the most intensively studied tornado disasters in U.S. history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: An episode about weathering storms and trusting that rain ends resonates with the memory of a devastating tornado outbreak and the communities that rebuilt afterward.

published.fact_text: On April 21, 1958, United Air Lines Flight 736 collided midair with a U.S. Air Force jet near Arden, Nevada. The crash, visible from the Las Vegas Strip, led to major reforms in air traffic control and how civilian and military aircraft share the sky.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: The eerie, cinematic feel of trip‑hop suits a night-sky collision that changed how we choreograph invisible routes in the air above cities.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2010, the Kharkiv Pact extended Russia’s lease on naval bases in Crimea in exchange for discounted natural gas to Ukraine. Signed with fanfare and street protests alike, the treaty became a flashpoint in the region’s complex energy and security story before Russia unilaterally terminated it in 2014.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A treaty about Eastern European borders and power aligns with an episode traveling through that same region’s culture and sound, hinting at the human landscapes behind geopolitical deals.

published.fact_text: On April 21, 1948, the United Nations Security Council adopted Resolution 47 on the Kashmir conflict, calling for a ceasefire, troop withdrawals, and a plebiscite. The ambitious plan revealed both the hope and the limits of early UN peacekeeping in one of the world’s most enduring territorial disputes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A complex international conflict and a UN attempt at mediation pair with an episode about perspective-shifting journeys and world sounds, suggesting the need for broader, calmer viewpoints.

published.fact_text: On April 21, 2014, Flint, Michigan switched its drinking water source from treated Lake Huron water to the Flint River as a cost-saving measure. Improper treatment corroded old pipes, leaching lead into households and turning a budget decision into a long-running public health catastrophe.

It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.
